sourceRefs.fallbackText: Requests like DELETE /posts/234 will fail if no other matching pattern is registered. In accordance with HTTP semantics , a net/http server will reply to such a request with a 405 Method Not Allowed error that lists the available methods in an Allow header.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Jonathan Amsterdam, on behalf of the Go team 13 February 2024
sourceRefs.textQuote: Jonathan Amsterdam, on behalf of the Go team 13 February 2024

highSignalBlocks.text: Jonathan Amsterdam, on behalf of the Go team 13 February 2024

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, it was a challenge to come up with the right rules for selecting the winning pattern when several match a request.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, i

highSignalBlocks.text: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, it was a challenge to come up with the right rules for selecting the winning pattern when several match a request.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We made these changes as part of our continuing effort to make Go a great language for building production systems. We studied many third-party web frameworks, extracted what we felt were the most used features, and integrated them into net/http . Then we validated our choices and improved our design by collaborating with the community in a GitHub discussion
sourceRefs.textQuote: We made these changes as part of our continuing effort to make Go a great language for building production systems. We studied many third-party web frameworks, extracted what we felt were the most used features, and inte

highSignalBlocks.text: We made these changes as part of our continuing effort to make Go a great language for building production systems. We studied many third-party web frameworks, extracted what we felt were the most used features, and integrated them into net/http . Then we validated our choices and improved our design by collaborating with the community in a GitHub discussion and a proposal issue . Adding these features to the standard library means one fewer dependency for many projects. But third-party web frameworks remain a fine choice for current users or programs with advanced routing needs.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The new routing features almost exclusively affect the pattern string passed to the two net/http.ServeMux methods Handle and HandleFunc , and the corresponding top-level functions http.Handle and http.HandleFunc . The only API changes are two new methods on net/http.Request for working with wildcard matches.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The new routing features almost exclusively affect the pattern string passed to the two net/http.ServeMux methods Handle and HandleFunc , and the corresponding top-level functions http.Handle and http.HandleFunc . The on

highSignalBlocks.text: The new routing features almost exclusively affect the pattern string passed to the two net/http.ServeMux methods Handle and HandleFunc , and the corresponding top-level functions http.Handle and http.HandleFunc . The only API changes are two new methods on net/http.Request for working with wildcard matches.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We’ll illustrate the changes with a hypothetical blog server in which every post has an integer identifier. A request like GET /posts/234 retrieves the post with ID 234. Before Go 1.22, the code for handling those requests would start with a line like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: We’ll illustrate the changes with a hypothetical blog server in which every post has an integer identifier. A request like GET /posts/234 retrieves the post with ID 234. Before Go 1.22, the code for handling those reques

highSignalBlocks.text: We’ll illustrate the changes with a hypothetical blog server in which every post has an integer identifier. A request like GET /posts/234 retrieves the post with ID 234. Before Go 1.22, the code for handling those requests would start with a line like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: http.HandleFunc("/posts/", handlePost)
sourceRefs.textQuote: http.HandleFunc("/posts/", handlePost)

highSignalBlocks.text: http.HandleFunc("/posts/", handlePost)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The trailing slash routes all requests beginning /posts/ to the handlePost function, which would have to check that the HTTP method was GET, extract the identifier, and retrieve the post. Since the method check isn’t strictly necessary to satisfy the request, it would be a natural mistake to omit it. That would mean that a request like DELETE /posts/234 woul
sourceRefs.textQuote: The trailing slash routes all requests beginning /posts/ to the handlePost function, which would have to check that the HTTP method was GET, extract the identifier, and retrieve the post. Since the method check isn’t str

highSignalBlocks.text: The trailing slash routes all requests beginning /posts/ to the handlePost function, which would have to check that the HTTP method was GET, extract the identifier, and retrieve the post. Since the method check isn’t strictly necessary to satisfy the request, it would be a natural mistake to omit it. That would mean that a request like DELETE /posts/234 would fetch the post, which is surprising at the least.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In Go 1.22, the existing code will continue to work, or you could instead write this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In Go 1.22, the existing code will continue to work, or you could instead write this:

highSignalBlocks.text: In Go 1.22, the existing code will continue to work, or you could instead write this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: http.HandleFunc("GET /posts/{id}", handlePost2)
sourceRefs.textQuote: http.HandleFunc("GET /posts/{id}", handlePost2)

highSignalBlocks.text: http.HandleFunc("GET /posts/{id}", handlePost2)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This pattern matches a GET request whose path begins “/posts/” and has two segments. (As a special case, GET also matches HEAD; all the other methods match exactly.) The handlePost2 function no longer needs to check the method, and extracting the identifier string can be written using the new PathValue method on Request :
sourceRefs.textQuote: This pattern matches a GET request whose path begins “/posts/” and has two segments. (As a special case, GET also matches HEAD; all the other methods match exactly.) The handlePost2 function no longer needs to check the

highSignalBlocks.text: This pattern matches a GET request whose path begins “/posts/” and has two segments. (As a special case, GET also matches HEAD; all the other methods match exactly.) The handlePost2 function no longer needs to check the method, and extracting the identifier string can be written using the new PathValue method on Request :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: idString := req.PathValue("id")
sourceRefs.textQuote: idString := req.PathValue("id")

highSignalBlocks.text: idString := req.PathValue("id")

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The rest of handlePost2 would behave like handlePost , converting the string identifier to an integer and fetching the post.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The rest of handlePost2 would behave like handlePost , converting the string identifier to an integer and fetching the post.

highSignalBlocks.text: The rest of handlePost2 would behave like handlePost , converting the string identifier to an integer and fetching the post.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A wildcard can match an entire segment, like {id} in the example above, or if it ends in ... it can match all the remaining segments of the path, as in the pattern /files/{pathname...} .
sourceRefs.textQuote: A wildcard can match an entire segment, like {id} in the example above, or if it ends in ... it can match all the remaining segments of the path, as in the pattern /files/{pathname...} .

highSignalBlocks.text: A wildcard can match an entire segment, like {id} in the example above, or if it ends in ... it can match all the remaining segments of the path, as in the pattern /files/{pathname...} .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is one last bit of syntax. As we showed above, patterns ending in a slash, like /posts/ , match all paths beginning with that string. To match only the path with the trailing slash, you can write /posts/{$} . That will match /posts/ but not /posts or /posts/234 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is one last bit of syntax. As we showed above, patterns ending in a slash, like /posts/ , match all paths beginning with that string. To match only the path with the trailing slash, you can write /posts/{$} . That

highSignalBlocks.text: There is one last bit of syntax. As we showed above, patterns ending in a slash, like /posts/ , match all paths beginning with that string. To match only the path with the trailing slash, you can write /posts/{$} . That will match /posts/ but not /posts or /posts/234 .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: And there is one last bit of API: net/http.Request has a SetPathValue method so that routers outside the standard library can make the results of their own path parsing available via Request.PathValue .
sourceRefs.textQuote: And there is one last bit of API: net/http.Request has a SetPathValue method so that routers outside the standard library can make the results of their own path parsing available via Request.PathValue .

highSignalBlocks.text: And there is one last bit of API: net/http.Request has a SetPathValue method so that routers outside the standard library can make the results of their own path parsing available via Request.PathValue .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Every HTTP router must deal with overlapping patterns, like /posts/{id} and /posts/latest . Both of these patterns match the path “posts/latest”, but at most one can serve the request. Which pattern takes precedence?
sourceRefs.textQuote: Every HTTP router must deal with overlapping patterns, like /posts/{id} and /posts/latest . Both of these patterns match the path “posts/latest”, but at most one can serve the request. Which pattern takes precedence?

highSignalBlocks.text: Every HTTP router must deal with overlapping patterns, like /posts/{id} and /posts/latest . Both of these patterns match the path “posts/latest”, but at most one can serve the request. Which pattern takes precedence?

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some routers disallow overlaps; others use the pattern that was registered last. Go has always allowed overlaps, and has chosen the longer pattern regardless of registration order. Preserving order-independence was important to us (and necessary for backwards compatibility), but we needed a better rule than “longest wins.” That rule would select /posts/lates
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some routers disallow overlaps; others use the pattern that was registered last. Go has always allowed overlaps, and has chosen the longer pattern regardless of registration order. Preserving order-independence was impor

highSignalBlocks.text: Some routers disallow overlaps; others use the pattern that was registered last. Go has always allowed overlaps, and has chosen the longer pattern regardless of registration order. Preserving order-independence was important to us (and necessary for backwards compatibility), but we needed a better rule than “longest wins.” That rule would select /posts/latest over /posts/{id} , but would choose /posts/{identifier} over both. That seems wrong: the wildcard name shouldn’t matter. It feels like /posts/latest should always win this competition, because it matches a single path instead of many.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Our quest for a good precedence rule led us to consider many properties of patterns. For example, we considered preferring the pattern with the longest literal (non-wildcard) prefix. That would choose /posts/latest over /posts/ {id} . But it wouldn’t distinguish between /users/{u}/posts/latest and /users/{u}/posts/{id} , and it seems like the former should t
sourceRefs.textQuote: Our quest for a good precedence rule led us to consider many properties of patterns. For example, we considered preferring the pattern with the longest literal (non-wildcard) prefix. That would choose /posts/latest over

highSignalBlocks.text: Our quest for a good precedence rule led us to consider many properties of patterns. For example, we considered preferring the pattern with the longest literal (non-wildcard) prefix. That would choose /posts/latest over /posts/ {id} . But it wouldn’t distinguish between /users/{u}/posts/latest and /users/{u}/posts/{id} , and it seems like the former should take precedence.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We eventually chose a rule based on what the patterns mean instead of how they look. Every valid pattern matches a set of requests. For example, /posts/latest matches requests with the path /posts/latest , while /posts/{id} matches requests with any two-segment path whose first segment is “posts”. We say that one pattern is more specific than another if it m
sourceRefs.textQuote: We eventually chose a rule based on what the patterns mean instead of how they look. Every valid pattern matches a set of requests. For example, /posts/latest matches requests with the path /posts/latest , while /posts/{

highSignalBlocks.text: We eventually chose a rule based on what the patterns mean instead of how they look. Every valid pattern matches a set of requests. For example, /posts/latest matches requests with the path /posts/latest , while /posts/{id} matches requests with any two-segment path whose first segment is “posts”. We say that one pattern is more specific than another if it matches a strict subset of requests. The pattern /posts/latest is more specific than /posts/{id} because the latter matches every request that the former does, and more.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The precedence rule is simple: the most specific pattern wins. This rule matches our intuition that posts/latests should be preferred to posts/{id} , and /users/{u}/posts/latest should be preferred to /users/{u}/posts/{id} . It also makes sense for methods. For example, GET /posts/{id} takes precedence over /posts/{id} because the first only matches GET and
sourceRefs.textQuote: The precedence rule is simple: the most specific pattern wins. This rule matches our intuition that posts/latests should be preferred to posts/{id} , and /users/{u}/posts/latest should be preferred to /users/{u}/posts/{i

highSignalBlocks.text: The precedence rule is simple: the most specific pattern wins. This rule matches our intuition that posts/latests should be preferred to posts/{id} , and /users/{u}/posts/latest should be preferred to /users/{u}/posts/{id} . It also makes sense for methods. For example, GET /posts/{id} takes precedence over /posts/{id} because the first only matches GET and HEAD requests, while the second matches requests with any method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The “most specific wins” rule generalizes the original “longest wins” rule for the path parts of original patterns, those without wildcards or {$} . Such patterns only overlap when one is a prefix of the other, and the longer is the more specific.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The “most specific wins” rule generalizes the original “longest wins” rule for the path parts of original patterns, those without wildcards or {$} . Such patterns only overlap when one is a prefix of the other, and the l

highSignalBlocks.text: The “most specific wins” rule generalizes the original “longest wins” rule for the path parts of original patterns, those without wildcards or {$} . Such patterns only overlap when one is a prefix of the other, and the longer is the more specific.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: What if two patterns overlap but neither is more specific? For example, /posts/{id} and /{resource}/latest both match /posts/latest . There is no obvious answer to which takes precedence, so we consider these patterns to conflict with each other. Registering both of them (in either order!) will panic.
sourceRefs.textQuote: What if two patterns overlap but neither is more specific? For example, /posts/{id} and /{resource}/latest both match /posts/latest . There is no obvious answer to which takes precedence, so we consider these patterns to

highSignalBlocks.text: What if two patterns overlap but neither is more specific? For example, /posts/{id} and /{resource}/latest both match /posts/latest . There is no obvious answer to which takes precedence, so we consider these patterns to conflict with each other. Registering both of them (in either order!) will panic.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The precedence rule works exactly as above for methods and paths, but we had to make one exception for hosts to preserve compatibility: if two patterns would otherwise conflict and one has a host while the other does not, then the pattern with the host takes precedence.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The precedence rule works exactly as above for methods and paths, but we had to make one exception for hosts to preserve compatibility: if two patterns would otherwise conflict and one has a host while the other does not

highSignalBlocks.text: The precedence rule works exactly as above for methods and paths, but we had to make one exception for hosts to preserve compatibility: if two patterns would otherwise conflict and one has a host while the other does not, then the pattern with the host takes precedence.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Students of computer science may recall the beautiful theory of regular expressions and regular languages. Each regular expression picks out a regular language, the set of strings matched by the expression. Some questions are easier to pose and answer by talking about languages rather than expressions. Our precedence rule was inspired by this theory. Indeed,
sourceRefs.textQuote: Students of computer science may recall the beautiful theory of regular expressions and regular languages. Each regular expression picks out a regular language, the set of strings matched by the expression. Some question

highSignalBlocks.text: Students of computer science may recall the beautiful theory of regular expressions and regular languages. Each regular expression picks out a regular language, the set of strings matched by the expression. Some questions are easier to pose and answer by talking about languages rather than expressions. Our precedence rule was inspired by this theory. Indeed, each routing pattern corresponds to a regular expression, and sets of matching requests play the role of regular languages.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Defining precedence by languages instead of expressions makes it easy to state and understand. But there is a downside to having a rule based on potentially infinite sets: it isn’t clear how to implement it efficiently. It turns out we can determine whether two patterns conflict by walking them segment by segment. Roughly speaking, if one pattern has a liter
sourceRefs.textQuote: Defining precedence by languages instead of expressions makes it easy to state and understand. But there is a downside to having a rule based on potentially infinite sets: it isn’t clear how to implement it efficiently.

highSignalBlocks.text: Defining precedence by languages instead of expressions makes it easy to state and understand. But there is a downside to having a rule based on potentially infinite sets: it isn’t clear how to implement it efficiently. It turns out we can determine whether two patterns conflict by walking them segment by segment. Roughly speaking, if one pattern has a literal segment wherever the other has a wildcard, it is more specific; but if literals align with wildcards in both directions, the patterns conflict.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As new patterns are registered on a ServeMux , it checks for conflicts with previously registered patterns. But checking every pair of patterns would take quadratic time. We use an index to skip patterns that cannot conflict with a new pattern; in practice, it works quite well. In any case, this check happens when patterns are registered, usually at server s
sourceRefs.textQuote: As new patterns are registered on a ServeMux , it checks for conflicts with previously registered patterns. But checking every pair of patterns would take quadratic time. We use an index to skip patterns that cannot conf

highSignalBlocks.text: As new patterns are registered on a ServeMux , it checks for conflicts with previously registered patterns. But checking every pair of patterns would take quadratic time. We use an index to skip patterns that cannot conflict with a new pattern; in practice, it works quite well. In any case, this check happens when patterns are registered, usually at server startup. The time to match incoming requests in Go 1.22 hasn’t changed much from previous versions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We made every effort to keep the new functionality compatible with older versions of Go. The new pattern syntax is a superset of the old, and the new precedence rule generalizes the old one. But there are a few edge cases. For example, previous versions of Go accepted patterns with braces and treated them literally, but Go 1.22 uses braces for wildcards. The
sourceRefs.textQuote: We made every effort to keep the new functionality compatible with older versions of Go. The new pattern syntax is a superset of the old, and the new precedence rule generalizes the old one. But there are a few edge case

highSignalBlocks.text: We made every effort to keep the new functionality compatible with older versions of Go. The new pattern syntax is a superset of the old, and the new precedence rule generalizes the old one. But there are a few edge cases. For example, previous versions of Go accepted patterns with braces and treated them literally, but Go 1.22 uses braces for wildcards. The GODEBUG setting httpmuxgo121 restores the old behavior.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For more details about these routing enhancements, see the net/http.ServeMux documentation .
sourceRefs.textQuote: For more details about these routing enhancements, see the net/http.ServeMux documentation .

highSignalBlocks.text: For more details about these routing enhancements, see the net/http.ServeMux documentation .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, it was a challenge to come up with the right rules for selecting the winning pattern when several match a request.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, it was a challenge to come up with the right rules for selecting the winning pattern when several match a request.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go 1.22 brings two enhancements to the net/http package’s router: method matching and wildcards. These features let you express common routes as patterns instead of Go code. Although they are simple to explain and use, i

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We made these changes as part of our continuing effort to make Go a great language for building production systems. We studied many third-party web frameworks, extracted what we felt were the most used features, and integrated them into net/http . Then we validated our choices and improved our design by collaborating with the community in a GitHub discussion
sourceRefs.textQuote: We made these changes as part of our continuing effort to make Go a great language for building production systems. We studied many third-party web frameworks, extracted what we felt were the most used features, and inte

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The new routing features almost exclusively affect the pattern string passed to the two net/http.ServeMux methods Handle and HandleFunc , and the corresponding top-level functions http.Handle and http.HandleFunc . The only API changes are two new methods on net/http.Request for working with wildcard matches.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The new routing features almost exclusively affect the pattern string passed to the two net/http.ServeMux methods Handle and HandleFunc , and the corresponding top-level functions http.Handle and http.HandleFunc . The on

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We’ll illustrate the changes with a hypothetical blog server in which every post has an integer identifier. A request like GET /posts/234 retrieves the post with ID 234. Before Go 1.22, the code for handling those requests would start with a line like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: We’ll illustrate the changes with a hypothetical blog server in which every post has an integer identifier. A request like GET /posts/234 retrieves the post with ID 234. Before Go 1.22, the code for handling those reques

sourceRefs.fallbackText: http.HandleFunc("/posts/", handlePost)
sourceRefs.textQuote: http.HandleFunc("/posts/", handlePost)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The trailing slash routes all requests beginning /posts/ to the handlePost function, which would have to check that the HTTP method was GET, extract the identifier, and retrieve the post. Since the method check isn’t strictly necessary to satisfy the request, it would be a natural mistake to omit it. That would mean that a request like DELETE /posts/234 woul
sourceRefs.textQuote: The trailing slash routes all requests beginning /posts/ to the handlePost function, which would have to check that the HTTP method was GET, extract the identifier, and retrieve the post. Since the method check isn’t str

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In Go 1.22, the existing code will continue to work, or you could instead write this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: In Go 1.22, the existing code will continue to work, or you could instead write this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: http.HandleFunc("GET /posts/{id}", handlePost2)
sourceRefs.textQuote: http.HandleFunc("GET /posts/{id}", handlePost2)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This pattern matches a GET request whose path begins “/posts/” and has two segments. (As a special case, GET also matches HEAD; all the other methods match exactly.) The handlePost2 function no longer needs to check the method, and extracting the identifier string can be written using the new PathValue method on Request :
sourceRefs.textQuote: This pattern matches a GET request whose path begins “/posts/” and has two segments. (As a special case, GET also matches HEAD; all the other methods match exactly.) The handlePost2 function no longer needs to check the

sourceRefs.fallbackText: idString := req.PathValue("id")
sourceRefs.textQuote: idString := req.PathValue("id")

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The rest of handlePost2 would behave like handlePost , converting the string identifier to an integer and fetching the post.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The rest of handlePost2 would behave like handlePost , converting the string identifier to an integer and fetching the post.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Requests like DELETE /posts/234 will fail if no other matching pattern is registered. In accordance with HTTP semantics , a net/http server will reply to such a request with a 405 Method Not Allowed error that lists the available methods in an Allow header.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Requests like DELETE /posts/234 will fail if no other matching pattern is registered. In accordance with HTTP semantics , a net/http server will reply to such a request with a 405 Method Not Allowed error that lists the

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A wildcard can match an entire segment, like {id} in the example above, or if it ends in ... it can match all the remaining segments of the path, as in the pattern /files/{pathname...} .
sourceRefs.textQuote: A wildcard can match an entire segment, like {id} in the example above, or if it ends in ... it can match all the remaining segments of the path, as in the pattern /files/{pathname...} .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There is one last bit of syntax. As we showed above, patterns ending in a slash, like /posts/ , match all paths beginning with that string. To match only the path with the trailing slash, you can write /posts/{$} . That will match /posts/ but not /posts or /posts/234 .
sourceRefs.textQuote: There is one last bit of syntax. As we showed above, patterns ending in a slash, like /posts/ , match all paths beginning with that string. To match only the path with the trailing slash, you can write /posts/{$} . That

sourceRefs.fallbackText: And there is one last bit of API: net/http.Request has a SetPathValue method so that routers outside the standard library can make the results of their own path parsing available via Request.PathValue .
sourceRefs.textQuote: And there is one last bit of API: net/http.Request has a SetPathValue method so that routers outside the standard library can make the results of their own path parsing available via Request.PathValue .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Every HTTP router must deal with overlapping patterns, like /posts/{id} and /posts/latest . Both of these patterns match the path “posts/latest”, but at most one can serve the request. Which pattern takes precedence?
sourceRefs.textQuote: Every HTTP router must deal with overlapping patterns, like /posts/{id} and /posts/latest . Both of these patterns match the path “posts/latest”, but at most one can serve the request. Which pattern takes precedence?

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some routers disallow overlaps; others use the pattern that was registered last. Go has always allowed overlaps, and has chosen the longer pattern regardless of registration order. Preserving order-independence was important to us (and necessary for backwards compatibility), but we needed a better rule than “longest wins.” That rule would select /posts/lates

For example, we considered preferring the pattern with the longest literal (non-wildcard) prefix. That would choose /posts/latest over /posts/ {id} . But it wouldn’t distinguish between /users/{u}/posts/latest and /users/{u}/posts/{id} , and it seems like the former should t

This rule matches our intuition that posts/latests should be preferred to posts/{id} , and /users/{u}/posts/latest should be preferred to /users/{u}/posts/{id} . It also makes sense for methods. sourceRefs.fallbackText: The “most specific wins” rule generalizes the original “longest wins” rule for the path parts of original patterns, those without wildcards or {$} . Such patterns only overlap when one is a prefix of the other, and the longer is the more specific.
